---
title: "1.5 Casting and Ranges of Values"
subtitle: "Part 2: Rounding, Ranges, and Precision"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Rounding

### Add or subtract `0.5`, then cast

Values of type `double` in the range that can be represented by an `int` can be rounded to the nearest `int`.

Add or subtract `0.5` and then cast the result with `(int)`.

```java
double number;    // positive value from somewhere
double negNumber; // negative value from somewhere

int nearestInt = (int)(number + 0.5);
int nearestNegInt = (int)(negNumber - 0.5);
```

## Rounding Example

### `1.75` and `1.25`

If you divide `7.0 / 4.0`, you get `1.75`.

If you cast that to an `int`, it will be truncated to `1`.

If you want to round a `double`, adding `0.5` gives `2.25`, which is then truncated to `2`.

For `5.0 / 4.2`, the result is about `1.25`; adding `0.5` gives `1.75`, which truncates back to `1`.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: nearestInt`

Textbook prompt: Run the code below to see how the formula of adding or subtracting `.5` and then casting with `(int)` rounds a positive or negative `double` number to the closest `int`.

Add a line of code that rounds `number + 2.3` to the nearest `int`.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: nearestInt`

```java
public class NearestInt
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        double number = 5.0 / 3;
        int nearestInt = (int) (number + 0.5);
        System.out.println("5.0/3 = " + number);
        System.out.println("5/3 truncated: " + (int) number);
        System.out.println("5.0/3 rounded to nearest int: " + nearestInt);
        double negNumber = -number;
        int nearestNegInt = (int) (negNumber - 0.5);
        System.out.println(
                "-5.0/3 rounded to nearest negative int: " + nearestNegInt);

        // Print the result of rounding (number + 2.3) to the nearest int.

    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: nearestInt`

Runestone expects this output from `main` after the added line:

```text
5.0/3 = 1.6666666666666667
5/3 truncated: 1
5.0/3 rounded to nearest int: 2
-5.0/3 rounded to nearest negative int: -2
4
```

## Range of Values

### `double` digits and `int` range

What happens to repeating decimal numbers like `3.333333` repeating?

Java limits the number of digits you can save for any `double` number to about `14-15` digits.

You should be aware that the accuracy of any calculation on a computer is limited by the fact that computers can only hold a limited number of digits.

## `int` Range

### Constants and overflow

`int` values are stored in `4` bytes of memory.

::: {.tight-list}
- `Integer.MAX_VALUE` holds `2147483647`.
- `Integer.MIN_VALUE` holds `-2147483648`.
- Values in an `int` variable must be within this inclusive range.
:::

If you try to store any number larger or smaller than these numbers in an `int` variable, it will result in an <span class="term">integer overflow</span>.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: overfl`

Textbook prompt: Try the code below to see two integer overflows for a positive and negative number.

An `int` cannot hold that many digits.

Fix the integer overflow by deleting the last `0` in the numbers to store fewer digits.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: overfl`

```java
public class TestOverflow
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int id = 2147483650; // overflow
        int negative = -2147483650; // overflow
        System.out.println(id);
        System.out.println(negative);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: overfl`

Runestone expects the fixed output:

```text
214748365
-214748365
```

## Double Precision

### Round-off error

Computers allot a specified amount of memory to store data based on the data type.

For `double` values, computers allot twice as much memory than for `int`s: `8` bytes rather than `4`.

It is still possible that an expression would evaluate to a mathematical value that requires more precision than can be stored in even `8` bytes.

In that case a <span class="term">round-off error</span> occurs.

## Double Precision

### About `15` digits

The result is rounded to the nearest `double` representable in `8` bytes.

For example, repeating decimals like `0.333333333333333333` will be cut off after about `15` digits.

To avoid round-off errors, you can choose to round the result to a specified number of digits after the decimal point or to an `int` value.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: double_precision`

Although it is not on the AP exam, you can format long decimal numbers to show `2` digits after the decimal point with `printf`.

Textbook prompt: Run the code below to see how a decimal number can be formatted to show `2` digits after the decimal point.

Try it with `2.0/3`.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: double_precision`

```java
public class TestFormat
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        double number = 10.0 / 3;
        // Note that this gets cut off after 15 digits
        System.out.println("Number cut off after 15 digits: " + number);
        // We could turn it into an int with some loss of precision
        System.out.println("Number as an int: " + (int) number);
        // Or format it to show 2 digits after .
        System.out.printf("Formatted number: %.2f", number);
        // format also with $ and newline
        System.out.printf("\nFormatted number: $%.2f\n", number);
        // Print out the result of 2.0/3 formatted to show 2 digits after the decimal point.

    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: double_precision`

Runestone checks that the output contains:

```text
0.67
```

for `2.0/3` formatted to show two digits after the decimal point.

## Check Your Understanding

### `mchoice:: q_round`

True or false: Java rounds up automatically when you do integer division.

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `true`
- B. `false`
:::

Correct answer: <span class="mark">B. `false`</span>

Java throws away any values after the decimal point if you do integer division. It does not round up automatically.

## Check Your Understanding

### `mchoice:: q_cast`

True or false: casting always results in a `double` type.

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `true`
- B. `false`
:::

Correct answer: <span class="mark">B. `false`</span>

Casting results in the type that you cast to. If you cannot really cast the value to the specified type, you will get an error.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- round positive `double` values with `(int)(number + 0.5)`
- round negative `double` values with `(int)(negNumber - 0.5)`
- identify `Integer.MAX_VALUE` and `Integer.MIN_VALUE`
- explain integer overflow when an `int` value is outside the allowed range
- describe `double` precision as limited to about `14-15` digits
- explain round-off error and formatting to a needed precision
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 3

[Next: Average 3 Numbers challenge →](1-5-part-3-average-3-numbers-challenge.html)
